allergies: penicillin — documented hives and throat swelling at age 22
patient.chief_complaint: sinus infection with facial pain and yellow-green nasal discharge

records.content: 45 y/o female referred for allergy evaluation. Patient reports history of allergic reaction to penicillin at age 22 — developed diffuse urticaria and throat tightening within 30 minutes of taking penicillin V for strep throat. Treated in ED with epinephrine and diphenhydramine. Classified as anaphylactic reaction. ALLERGY: PENICILLIN — ANAPHYLAXIS. Skin prick testing today confirms penicillin allergy. Advised strict avoidance of all penicillin-class antibiotics including amoxicillin, ampicillin, and piperacillin. Cross-reactivity with cephalosporins possible (1-2% risk). Assessment: Confirmed penicillin allergy with history of anaphylaxis. Plan: Updated allergy list in chart. Patient educated on cross-reactive drugs.

ALLERGY ALERT — PENICILLIN: Reaction type: Anaphylaxis. Severity: SEVERE. Onset: Immediate (within 30 min). Symptoms: Urticaria, angioedema, throat tightening. Required epinephrine. Date of reaction: 1999. Confirmed by: skin prick test 2021-06-10. Cross-reactive drugs to avoid: amoxicillin, ampicillin, piperacillin/tazobactam. Use with caution: 1st generation cephalosporins.

45 y/o female presents for annual wellness visit. PMH: asthma (well-controlled), penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis). Current meds: albuterol PRN, fluticasone 250mcg BID. Vitals: BP 118/74, HR 72, RR 16, Temp 98.4°F, BMI 24.1. HEENT: normal. Lungs: clear bilateral, no wheezing. CV: RRR, no murmurs. Abd: soft, non-tender. Skin: no rashes. Assessment: Healthy female, asthma well-controlled. Plan: Continue current medications. Flu vaccine administered. Routine labs ordered.

45 y/o female presents with 5-day history of nasal congestion, sore throat, and mild cough. No fever. Lungs clear. Pharynx erythematous without exudate. Assessment: Viral URI. Plan: Supportive care — rest, fluids, acetaminophen PRN. Discussed antibiotic stewardship — no antibiotics indicated for viral URI. Follow up if symptoms worsen or persist > 10 days.

45 y/o female with asthma presents for routine follow-up. Reports using albuterol rescue inhaler about once per week. No nocturnal symptoms. No ED visits or hospitalizations. PEF 420 L/min (95% predicted). Assessment: Asthma, well-controlled. Plan: Continue fluticasone 250mcg BID and albuterol PRN. Annual flu vaccine next fall.

45 y/o female presents with right knee pain x 2 weeks after hiking. No swelling, no locking. ROM slightly decreased. McMurray test negative. Assessment: Likely mild patellar tendinitis. Plan: Rest, ice, compression, elevation. Acetaminophen 500mg PRN for pain. Physical therapy referral if not improved in 4 weeks.

46 y/o female for annual wellness. PMH: asthma, penicillin allergy. Vitals: BP 122/76, HR 70, BMI 24.3. All systems reviewed — no concerns. Lungs clear. Assessment: Healthy. Plan: Routine labs, flu vaccine, continue meds.

46 y/o female returns for follow-up of right knee pain. Reports complete resolution after 3 weeks of rest and ice. No current pain, full ROM. Assessment: Resolved patellar tendinitis. Plan: Resume normal activities, return if recurs.

46 y/o female presents with recurrent tension-type headaches x 3 weeks. Bilateral, band-like pressure. No visual changes, no nausea, no neurological deficits. Stress-related (work deadline). Vitals normal. Assessment: Tension-type headaches. Plan: Ibuprofen 400mg PRN (max 3 days/week to avoid rebound), stress management, adequate sleep. Follow up if worsening.
46 y/o female with asthma. Stable. Using rescue inhaler less than once weekly. No exacerbations. PEF 415 L/min. Assessment: Well-controlled asthma. Plan: Continue current regimen.
47 y/o female for annual wellness. PMH: asthma, penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis). Vitals: BP 120/78, HR 68, BMI 24.5. All WNL. Assessment: Healthy female. Plan: Routine labs, flu vaccine.

47 y/o female presents with contact dermatitis on both hands — likely from new cleaning products. No systemic symptoms. Assessment: Allergic contact dermatitis. Plan: Avoid offending agent, hydrocortisone 1% cream topically BID x 2 weeks, moisturize frequently.
47 y/o female with asthma. Well-controlled. No exacerbations. Refills requested for albuterol and fluticasone. Assessment: Stable asthma. Plan: Refills provided.

47 y/o female presents to urgent care with 10-day history of facial pain/pressure, purulent nasal discharge (yellow-green), and low-grade fever (100.2°F). Symptoms initially improved then worsened over past 3 days. No improvement with OTC decongestants. PMH: asthma. Vitals: BP 124/78, HR 76, Temp 100.2°F. HEENT: Maxillary sinus tenderness bilateral, purulent drainage in posterior pharynx. Lungs: clear. Assessment: Acute bacterial sinusitis. Plan: Amoxicillin 500mg TID x 10 days. Nasal saline irrigation. Acetaminophen PRN for pain/fever. Follow up in 10-14 days if not improved.

Rx# 4482910 — Amoxicillin 500mg capsules, qty 30, sig: take one capsule three times daily for 10 days. Prescriber: Dr. James Wong. Patient: P-10042. Dispensed by Valley Pharmacy. No drug interaction alerts triggered in pharmacy system. Patient counseled on completing full course.

47 y/o female follows up after 10-day course of amoxicillin for sinusitis. Reports sinusitis resolved. However, on day 3 of antibiotics, developed mild itching on arms — took diphenhydramine, itching resolved. Did not develop hives, swelling, or breathing difficulty. Completed full course. Assessment: Resolved sinusitis. Note: Patient tolerated amoxicillin this time with only mild itching, but given her DOCUMENTED PENICILLIN ALLERGY WITH HISTORY OF ANAPHYLAXIS, this prescription should not have been written. This is a NEAR-MISS event. Plan: Incident report filed. Patient re-educated about her allergy. Allergy alert re-confirmed in chart. Future antibiotic needs: azithromycin or fluoroquinolones (with caution given asthma).

ground_truth_issues.description: Amoxicillin (a penicillin-class antibiotic) prescribed to a patient with documented severe penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis). Amoxicillin is cross-reactive with penicillin. This could have caused a life-threatening anaphylactic reaction.
ground_truth_issues.clinical_reference: Penicillin allergy cross-reactivity — amoxicillin shares the beta-lactam ring structure with penicillin. Patients with history of penicillin anaphylaxis should NEVER receive amoxicillin.
ground_truth_issues.what_should_have_happened: Dr. Wong should have checked allergy list before prescribing. Alternative antibiotics for sinusitis in penicillin-allergic patients: doxycycline, azithromycin, or respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin).
